skill.name: sprint-retrospective
skill.description: Facilitate effective sprint retrospectives for continuous team improvement. Use when conducting team retrospectives, identifying improvements, or fostering team collaboration. Handles retrospective formats, action items, and facilitation techniques.
skill.summary: Guide teams through structured retrospective sessions with proven formats like Start-Stop-Continue, Mad-Sad-Glad, and 4Ls.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 20 potential security issues, but evaluation confirms all are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill is a pure markdown documentation file containing retrospective templates. No executable code, command injection, cryptography, or malicious network activity exists. Markdown code block delimiters were misidentified as backtick shell execution. English words in templates were misidentified as cryptographic algorithms. Reference URLs are legitimate documentation links. Safe for publication.

dangerous_patterns.title: Misidentified: Markdown code blocks as backtick execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer incorrectly flagged markdown code block delimiters (```) as Ruby/shell backtick execution. SKILL.md contains only documentation templates - no executable code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Evidence confirmed: all flagged locations are markdown code block fences (```), not backtick system calls. Pattern match without semantic context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Misidentified: English words as cryptographic algorithms
dangerous_patterns.description: Static analyzer flagged words like 'long' (from 'longed for'), 'review', and 'design' as weak cryptographic algorithms. These are plain English words in retrospective templates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed false positive: 'long' appears in 'LONGED FOR' section header, 'review' in 'pre-review process', 'design' in 'design changes' - none related to cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: Misidentified: Reference URLs as network exfiltration
dangerous_patterns.description: Hardcoded URLs at lines 150-151 are legitimate documentation references (retromat.org, Amazon book) in a documentation file, not malicious network calls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed benign: URLs point to retromat.org (retrospective formats) and Amazon (Agile Retrospectives book) - standard documentation references.

dangerous_patterns.title: Misidentified: Requirements change process as network reconnaissance
dangerous_patterns.description: The phrase 'Improve the requirements change process' at line 69 was flagged as network reconnaissance. This is a normal retrospective action item.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed benign: text describes a process improvement action item in a retrospective template, not reconnaissance activity.

content.user_title: Facilitate Sprint Retrospectives with Structured Templates
content.value_statement: Teams struggle to run effective retrospectives that produce actionable outcomes. This skill provides proven retrospective formats and facilitation guidance to help teams continuously improve their processes.
actual_capabilities: Guide teams through Start-Stop-Continue retrospective format
Generate structured retrospective documents with action items
Enforce facilitation rules for safe, productive sessions
Create follow-up tasks with assignees and deadlines
limitations: Cannot attend or facilitate live sessions
Cannot track action item completion across sprints
Does not integrate with project management tools
Templates require human facilitation for team participation

use_cases.title: End-of-Sprint Review
use_cases.description: Generate a complete retrospective document at sprint end using the Start-Stop-Continue format to capture team feedback and create actionable improvement items.

use_cases.title: Post-Release Reflection
use_cases.description: Use the Mad-Sad-Glad format after major releases to understand team emotions and identify both successes and areas for improvement.

use_cases.title: Team Health Check
use_cases.description: Run the 4Ls retrospective to assess team dynamics, learning, gaps, and aspirations over a quarterly period.

prompt_templates.title: Quick Sprint Retrospective
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a sprint retrospective for our team. We just finished sprint 15. Use the Start-Stop-Continue format. Our team had 5 members and we completed 25 story points.

prompt_templates.title: Emotional Team Check-in
prompt_templates.prompt: We had a challenging sprint with several production incidents. Help us run a Mad-Sad-Glad retrospective to process what happened and identify how to prevent future issues.

prompt_templates.title: Quarterly Team Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Help us run a comprehensive 4Ls retrospective to reflect on the past quarter. We want to capture what we liked, learned, lacked, and longed for as a team.

prompt_templates.title: Action Item Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Based on our retrospective notes: [paste notes], create a prioritized list of action items with specific owners and target dates. Limit to the top 3 most impactful items.

output_examples.input: Run a Start-Stop-Continue retrospective for sprint 12. Our team is 4 people. We hit our velocity of 20 points but had 3 bugs escape to production.
output_examples.output: A complete retrospective document with START items (new practices to adopt), STOP items (harmful behaviors to eliminate), CONTINUE items (what worked well), and specific action items with owners and deadlines.

output_examples.input: Generate a Mad-Sad-Glad retrospective after our major release. We had stress around deployment but customers gave positive feedback.
output_examples.output: A structured retrospective capturing team emotions, highlighting concerns about the deployment process, sadness about stress levels, and gladness about customer success, with concrete follow-up actions.

output_examples.input: Create action items from our 4Ls retro: Liked new tech stack adoption, Learned Docker Compose setup, Lacked documentation time, Longed for external training.
output_examples.output: Three prioritized action items: schedule Docker documentation sessions, propose external training budget for next quarter, and celebrate tech stack wins with the team.

best_practices: Time-box retrospectives to 60 minutes maximum to maintain focus and engagement
Limit action items to 2-3 per retrospective to ensure meaningful follow-through
Rotate the facilitator role among team members to build shared ownership
anti_patterns: Do not skip retrospectives when the sprint went well - consistent reflection builds team habits
Do not assign too many action items - spread improvements across multiple sprints
Do not allow personal attacks - focus feedback on processes and systems, not individuals

faq.question: What retrospective format should I use?
faq.answer: Start-Stop-Continue works well for regular sprints. Mad-Sad-Glad is effective after emotional events. Use 4Ls for comprehensive quarterly reviews.

faq.question: How many action items should we create?
faq.answer: Limit to 2-3 action items per retrospective. Fewer items with clear owners and deadlines are more likely to be completed than long lists.

faq.question: How long should a retrospective take?
faq.answer: Keep retrospectives to 60 minutes maximum. For teams larger than 8 people, consider breakout groups to ensure everyone participates.

faq.question: Can this skill replace a human facilitator?
faq.answer: This skill generates templates and documentation. A human facilitator is still needed to guide discussion, manage time, and ensure psychological safety.

faq.question: How do we track action item completion?
faq.answer: Review action item status at the start of each retrospective. Create tickets in your project management tool for automated tracking if needed.

faq.question: What if team members are reluctant to share?
faq.answer: Use anonymous input collection first, then discuss as a group. Start with what went well to build trust before addressing challenges.
